text: The surgeon uses the forearm da Vinci SI robot with three 8mm and two 12mm trocars. The patient is in a supine position with the table in reverse Trendelenburg and a slight right tilt. Arm number three serves as a liver retractor. The surgeon exposes the hiatus and reduces the hernia using a double fenestrated grasper in arm number two. The hernia sac is dissected with an advanced bipolar device, starting from the left side of the hiatus and separating it circumferentially from mediastinal and pleural attachments, taking care to avoid the pleural cavity.

The assistant retracts the stomach down and laterally while the surgeon divides the frontal esophageal ligament using an advanced bipolar device. Dissection continues around the gastroesophageal junction, creating a retroesophageal window through gentle blunt dissection. A Penrose drain encircles the esophagus for non-traumatic retraction. The phrenoesophageal ligament is completely divided, allowing circumferential mobilization of the distal esophagus to achieve adequate intra-abdominal esophageal length.

The surgeon closes the hiatus posteriorly using interrupted stitches with zero silk, placing each suture one centimeter apart. A calibration bougie is used to ensure the closure is not too tight. Bioabsorbable pledges are included for reinforcement, and the slipped knot technique is applied, following standard suturing principles.

The surgeon uses regular laparoscopic scissors to cut sutures, avoiding the use of robotic shears. The anterior closure is adjusted by removing a constricting stitch and placing an additional suture posteriorly. An intraoperative endoscopy is performed to verify the passage of the endoscope, assess the GEJ position, intraabdominal esophageal length, and mucosal integrity. A pneumatic leak test is conducted, confirming no fundoplication due to ineffective esophageal motility.
A round-blade drain is placed in the mediastinum. The surgeon performs a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy, followed by closure of the port sites using standard techniques.
